frames.caption: Initialize converging pointers: left = 0, right = 11. leftMax = rightMax = water = 0.
frames.note: Water above bar i is min(maxLeft, maxRight) − height[i]. The two-pointer trick avoids the O(n)-space prefix/suffix arrays: by walking inward from both ends and tracking only the running maxima leftMax and rightMax, we can fix each cell's water the moment its shorter wall is determined. left and right enclose the still-unknown region; everything outside is already accounted for.

frames.caption: hL=0 < hR=1: move L. leftMax=0, add 0 -> water=0. left -> 1.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: hL=1 = hR=1: move R. rightMax=1, add 0 -> water=0. right -> 10.
frames.note: height[left] >= height[right], so the RIGHT wall is the bottleneck (ties go right to match the reviewer). By the mirror of the same argument, leftMax is already ≥ height[left] ≥ height[right], so the water above right is fixed at rightMax − height[right] regardless of the unseen middle. Bank it and advance right. Always moving the shorter side is what makes one O(1)-space pass correct.

frames.caption: hL=1 < hR=2: move L. leftMax=1, add 0 -> water=0. left -> 2.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: hL=0 < hR=2: move L. leftMax=1, add 1 -> water=1. left -> 3.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: hL=2 = hR=2: move R. rightMax=2, add 0 -> water=1. right -> 9.
frames.note: height[left] >= height[right], so the RIGHT wall is the bottleneck (ties go right to match the reviewer). By the mirror of the same argument, leftMax is already ≥ height[left] ≥ height[right], so the water above right is fixed at rightMax − height[right] regardless of the unseen middle. Bank it and advance right. Always moving the shorter side is what makes one O(1)-space pass correct.

frames.caption: hL=2 > hR=1: move R. rightMax=2, add 1 -> water=2. right -> 8.
frames.note: height[left] >= height[right], so the RIGHT wall is the bottleneck (ties go right to match the reviewer). By the mirror of the same argument, leftMax is already ≥ height[left] ≥ height[right], so the water above right is fixed at rightMax − height[right] regardless of the unseen middle. Bank it and advance right. Always moving the shorter side is what makes one O(1)-space pass correct.

frames.caption: hL=2 = hR=2: move R. rightMax=2, add 0 -> water=2. right -> 7.
frames.note: height[left] >= height[right], so the RIGHT wall is the bottleneck (ties go right to match the reviewer). By the mirror of the same argument, leftMax is already ≥ height[left] ≥ height[right], so the water above right is fixed at rightMax − height[right] regardless of the unseen middle. Bank it and advance right. Always moving the shorter side is what makes one O(1)-space pass correct.

frames.caption: hL=2 < hR=3: move L. leftMax=2, add 0 -> water=2. left -> 4.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: hL=1 < hR=3: move L. leftMax=2, add 1 -> water=3. left -> 5.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: hL=0 < hR=3: move L. leftMax=2, add 2 -> water=5. left -> 6.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: hL=1 < hR=3: move L. leftMax=2, add 1 -> water=6. left -> 7.
frames.note: height[left] < height[right], so the LEFT wall is the bottleneck. Here is the key insight: because some bar on the right is taller than height[left], rightMax is already guaranteed ≥ that bar > height[left] — so the water above left is fixed at leftMax − height[left] no matter what we have not seen yet on the far side. We bank that water and advance left, shrinking the unknown region.

frames.caption: left met right at index 7 — the unknown region is empty. Every bar's water has been counted: total = 6.
frames.note: When left and right collide there is no interior left to fill, so the accumulated water is the answer. We touched each bar once and used only a handful of scalars: O(n) time, O(1) space — strictly better than the prefix/suffix-array solution's O(n) space, and the canonical reason interviewers reach for this problem to test the converging two-pointer idea.
